title: PostgreSQL Connection Pool Exhaustion Remediation Workflow
triggers_and_scope:
1. Critical Alert: 'PostgreSQL Connection Count Exceeded 95% of max_connections'.
2. Application Logs: 'org.postgresql.util.PSQLException: FATAL: remaining connection slots are reserved for non-replication superuser connections'.
3. HikariCP Pool Logs: 'booking-service-pool - Connection is not available, request timed out after 30000ms'.
4. Scope: Affects the checkout, confirmation, and room-allocation workflows across the entire 'booking-service' microservice cluster.
issue_summary: Under intense transactional load (e.g., flash sales or peak morning checkout windows), the 'booking-service' cluster exhausts its allocated connection limits to the primary PostgreSQL database instance. This is primarily caused by unclosed connection objects within the 'billing-orchestrator' sub-module or long-running analytics queries blocking the transactional pool. When the pool starves, the application can no longer fulfill basic database read/write requests, cascading into widespread HTTP 500 Internal Server Errors across all client-facing applications.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Isolate the source of the leaking connections by executing an analytical query against the system catalog. Run the following command: 'SELECT pid, age(clock_timestamp(), query_start), usename, query, state FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state != 'idle' AND age(clock_timestamp(), query_start) > interval '30 seconds' ORDER BY age DESC;'
Step 2: Identify rogue or hanging connections categorized as 'idle in transaction' that have persisted for more than 60 seconds.
Step 3: Terminate the offending backend processes safely without restarting the engine using the process ID discovered in Step 1: 'SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE pid = <TARGET_PID>;'
Step 4: Scale up the intermediate connection pooling layer (PgBouncer) to absorb the traffic burst. Modify the PgBouncer configuration file located at '/etc/pgbouncer/pgbouncer.ini' and update the parameter: 'max_client_conn = 5000' and 'default_pool_size = 150'.
Step 5: Force PgBouncer to reload its configuration non-destructively: 'kill -HUP $(pgrep pgbouncer)' or execute 'RELOAD;' within the PgBouncer administrative console.
Step 6: Verify connection availability by monitoring the active connection counts over a 5-minute window: 'SELECT count(*), state FROM pg_stat_activity GROUP BY state;'
Step 7: Confirm that the application layer recovery is underway by inspecting the 'booking-service' container logs for successful connection pool acquisition metrics.

title: Evicting Stale Distributed Room Inventory Locks in Redis Cluster
triggers_and_scope:
1. Telemetry Alert: 'Redis Transaction Contention Rate > 40%'.
2. Application Logs: 'com.hotel.exception.RedisLockException: Could not acquire distributed lock for Room Type Resource ID: RM-XYZ-102'.
3. Front-End Symptoms: Customers encounter a persistent 'Room mapping locked by another transaction' error message during the room-selection and holding phase.
4. Scope: Confined to the 'inventory-manager' microservice and the global Redis caching layer.
issue_summary: The application utilizes Redisson-based distributed locks to prevent double-booking of identical room assets during parallel checkout sequences. If an instance of the 'inventory-manager' service undergoes an unhandled crash or a sudden Kubernetes eviction while holding an active inventory lock, the lock key may become orphaned in Redis if the configured Time-To-Live (TTL) parameter fails to register. Consequently, that specific room asset remains indefinitely blocked from further reservations, degrading available room counts despite physical vacancy.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Establish a secure interactive terminal session with the primary production Redis cluster node: 'redis-cli -h redis-prod-cluster.internal.net -p 6379 -a $REDIS_AUTH_TOKEN'
Step 2: Inspect the target keyspace to identify the exact distributed lock keys currently active without a proper expiration threshold. Execute a safe scan operation: 'SCAN 0 MATCH lock:room:* COUNT 1000'
Step 3: Query the remaining Time-To-Live for the suspect orphaned keys returned by the scan: 'TTL lock:room:RM-XYZ-102'. If the command returns '-1', it definitively confirms that the key has no expiration set and is permanently blocking the inventory pipeline.
Step 4: Remove the stale blocking lock key from the distributed database cluster to free the resource: 'DEL lock:room:RM-XYZ-102'
Step 5: If multiple keys are impacted, execute a atomic pipeline deletion using the system shell to avoid freezing the single-threaded Redis engine: 'redis-cli -h redis-prod-cluster.internal.net --scan --pattern "lock:room:*" | xargs redis-cli -h redis-prod-cluster.internal.net DEL'
Step 6: Force the 'inventory-manager' service to synchronize its in-memory metrics with the state of the relational database database by invoking the cache eviction administration endpoint: 'curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN" https://inventory-manager.internal.net/api/v1/cache/flush-stale'
Step 7: Validate system recovery by monitoring the application log streams for successful, non-blocking lock acquisitions.

title: Stripe Payment Gateway Latency Mitigation and Circuit Breaker Toggling
triggers_and_scope:
1. API Alert: 'Outbound Gateway Error Rate Exceeded 15%'.
2. Logs: 'java.net.SocketTimeoutException: Read timed out to api.stripe.com' accompanied by Resilience4j state transitions stating 'CircuitBreaker payment-gateway-cb CHANGED_STATE FROM CLOSED TO OPEN'.
3. Scope: Impacts the 'payment-gateway' service, completely halting room purchases, deposit collections, and invoice settlements.
issue_summary: Upstream infrastructure degradation or regional connectivity issues at our primary payment processor (Stripe) cause transaction latency to exceed our strict 5000ms SLA limit. As outbound application threads bottleneck waiting for responses, connection pools saturate, risking a cluster-wide failure of the payment subsystem. The automated circuit breaker trips to protect internal services, but manual operator intervention is required to reroute traffic to the secondary fallback processor (Adyen) to restore booking conversions.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Assess external platform status by executing a direct test payload from the production environment to verify current network transit times: 'curl -w "@curl-format.txt" -o /dev/null -s https://api.stripe.com/v1/charges'
Step 2: Access the central runtime environment configuration parameters for the 'payment-gateway' service deployment via the configuration server console.
Step 3: Switch the active billing traffic routing matrix to the secondary payment partner by flipping the fallback environment variable: 'export GATEWAY_PRIMARY_PROCESSOR="adyen"' and 'export USE_FALLBACK_PAYMENT=true'.
Step 4: Send a hang-up signal (SIGHUP) to the running payment service worker processes to trigger a hot-reload of the configuration values without terminating active network sockets: 'kill -HUP $(pgrep payment-gateway-service)'
Step 5: If hot-reloading fails to apply the configuration, execute a rapid rolling update of the payment container infrastructure: 'kubectl rollout restart deployment/payment-gateway-service -n production'
Step 6: Monitor the real-time application logs to ensure the circuit breaker resets and transitions cleanly back to functional status: 'kubectl logs -f deployment/payment-gateway-service -n production | grep -E "CircuitBreaker|fallback"'
Step 7: Execute a validation transaction using a production-safe test profile to ensure that transaction flows are processing via the alternative provider infrastructure.

title: Resolving Pod CrashLoopBackOff Due to JVM OOMKilled in Reservation Service
triggers_and_scope:
1. Cluster Event Alert: 'Kubernetes Pod OOMKilled Detected'.
2. Pod Status: 'kubectl get pods' outputs Status: 'CrashLoopBackOff' and Reason: 'OOMKilled' (Exit Code 137).
3. Logs: 'java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space' followed by immediate container termination logs.
4. Scope: Limits the execution of the night-audit processing, daily room cleaning allocation logs, and mass PDF invoice compilation engine.
issue_summary: During the generation of large-scale corporate account occupancy reports or the execution of the automated midnight system audit, the 'reservation-service' is forced to load massive datasets into memory. If the size of the retrieved database entities exceeds the maximum designated Java heap allocation (`-Xmx`), the Linux kernel out-of-memory (OOM) killer steps in and forcefully terminates the container process with exit code 137. This causes a loop where the container repeatedly boots, attempts to resume the report task, and crashes.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Check the cluster events to isolate the exact node and timeframe of the termination: 'kubectl describe pod -l app=reservation-service -n production'
Step 2: Instantly scale out the deployment horizontally to distribute incoming traffic and isolate the processing node that is attempting to execute the corrupted report: 'kubectl scale deployment reservation-service --replicas=6 -n production'
Step 3: Modify the container definition files to dynamically increase the available memory limits and Java heap space configurations. Edit the active deployment spec using: 'kubectl edit deployment reservation-service -n production'
Step 4: Locate the 'resources.limits.memory' configuration block and increase it from '1Gi' to '2Gi'. Concurrently, update the application environment variables controlling the JVM parameters: 'JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS="-Xms1024m -Xmx1536m -XX:+UseG1GC"'
Step 5: Save and apply the structural modification, which will automatically initiate a safe, progressive rolling update across the pod cluster environment.
Step 6: Track the deployment status to ensure all new pods achieve a continuous 'Running' state: 'kubectl rollout status deployment/reservation-service -n production'
Step 7: Extract the generated heap dump profile from the persistent storage volume for future profiling: 'kubectl cp production/reservation-service-old:/tmp/heap_dump.hprof ./heap_dump.hprof'

title: Disk Space Saturation Mitigation on Application Node Log Volumes
triggers_and_scope:
1. Infrastructure Metrics: 'Host Storage Utilization Alert: /var/log exceeding 96% availability limits'.
2. Server System Logs: 'IOError: [Errno 28] No space left on device' written across active daemon output handlers.
3. Scope: Impact is widespread, affecting log shippers (Fluentd), local diagnostics capturing, and file-based caching systems on node instances.
issue_summary: An engineer accidentally leaves an application component configured to run at the 'TRACE' or 'DEBUG' log verbosity level following a complex troubleshooting session. This causes rapid log inflation, where gigabytes of raw textual metrics fill the local node storage blocks. When disk space saturates completely, the operating system can no longer allocate tracking space for basic operational processes, causing unexpected service drops and corrupted local file systems.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Establish an encrypted SSH administrative terminal session directly to the impacted host file system: 'ssh administrator@node-04.internal.hotelapp.net'
Step 2: Evaluate directory sizes on the root volume to isolate the specific folder consuming block storage arrays: 'df -h' followed by 'du -sh /var/log/hotel-app/* | sort -rh | head -n 5'
Step 3: Isolate active uncompressed files that are filling the partition blocks and check their current write speed: 'tail -f /var/log/hotel-app/booking-engine-trace.log'
Step 4: Do NOT delete active files with open file descriptors, as this will not release disk space. Instead, safely truncate the running log file to instantly reclaim storage blocks: 'cat /dev/null > /var/log/hotel-app/booking-engine-trace.log'
Step 5: Locate and delete old, compressed log archives older than 48 hours to create immediate breathing room on the system partition: 'find /var/log/hotel-app/ -name "*.log.gz" -mtime +2 -exec rm -f {} \;'
Step 6: Access the service configuration manager tool or update the local properties file to restore standard production log levels: 'sed -i "s/logging.level.root=TRACE/logging.level.root=WARN/g" /etc/hotel-app/application.properties'
Step 7: Execute a safe configuration cycle on the system logging facility to ensure automated rotation functions perfectly moving forward: 'systemctl restart logrotate' followed by 'systemctl reload hotel-booking-service'

title: RB-006: JWT Cryptographic Verification Key Desynchronization Recovery
triggers_and_scope:
1. App Security Alerts: 'High Frequency Security Failure Rate: Invalid Signature Alerts'.
2. Logs: 'io.jsonwebtoken.SignatureException: JWT signature does not match locally cached credentials'.
3. Front-End Symptoms: Valid customers are instantly redirected back to the login screen or encounter HTTP 401 Unauthorized errors when modifying reservations.
4. Scope: Impacts authentication checks executed by the 'booking-service' on tokens generated by the 'auth-service'.
issue_summary: To maximize execution performance, the 'booking-service' caches the public JSON Web Key Sets (JWKS) exposed by our 'auth-service' identity server inside local memory structures. When the 'auth-service' executes its automated cryptographic key rotation policy, it publishes a new set of signing credentials. If the internal caching mechanism of the 'booking-service' fails to invalidate its historic key matrix, it will assess incoming user credentials against expired keys, failing valid authentication assertions.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Query the 'auth-service' JWKS public endpoint directly using network diagnostic utilities to verify the identity of the currently active key identifiers: 'curl -s https://auth-service.internal.net/.well-known/jwks.json | jq ".keys[].kid"'
Step 2: Inspect the configuration details of the 'booking-service' cluster instances to determine the last recorded cache eviction timestamp.
Step 3: Force an immediate flush of the authentication cache layer by dispatching an authenticated management POST request to the application's internal actuator endpoint: 'curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $SYSTEM_INTERNAL_KEY" https://booking-service.internal.net/actuator/clearcache/auth-keys'
Step 4: If the custom cache clearing endpoint fails to respond or execute successfully due to internal state corruption, perform a zero-downtime rolling deployment of the microservice pods to force an initialization state fetch: 'kubectl rollout restart deployment/booking-service -n production'
Step 5: Track the deployment rollout to ensure smooth system processing: 'kubectl rollout status deployment/booking-service -n production'
Step 6: Examine incoming request log entries to ensure that validation failures drop back to normal thresholds: 'kubectl logs -l app=booking-service -n production --tail=100 | grep -E "JWT|410|401"'
Step 7: Execute an automated integration end-to-end check simulating a user login and subsequent account retrieval profile query.

title: Elasticsearch Cluster Red Status and Shard Allocation Remediation
triggers_and_scope:
1. System Monitoring Alert: 'Elasticsearch Cluster Health Status: RED'.
2. Logs: 'org.elasticsearch.indices.IndexPrimaryShardNotAllocatedException: [hotel_rooms][2] primary shard is unassigned'.
3. Front-End Symptoms: The hotel search bar, location mapping interface, and filter components fail entirely, returning zero available properties or timing out.
4. Scope: Isolated to the 'search-api-service' and the underlying Elasticsearch data persistence layer.
issue_summary: An underlying infrastructure storage virtualization fault or unexpected hardware termination causes an active Elasticsearch data node to disconnect from the cluster. Because the index 'hotel_rooms' was processing heavy ingestion routines without fully mirrored replication targets across separate physical volumes at that exact moment, primary data shards fall into an unassigned, offline state. When an index loses a primary shard, the global cluster state drops to RED, blocking all search read/write operations.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Check the cluster health metrics to isolate the total count of unassigned or corrupted storage shards: 'curl -X GET "localhost:9200/_cluster/health?pretty"'
Step 2: Request an allocation explanation report from the engine to discover why the specific shards cannot mount or recover automatically: 'curl -X GET "localhost:9200/_cluster/allocation/explain?pretty"'
Step 3: Identify the specific node name and unassigned shard ID from the payload output structure.
Step 4: Execute an administrative routing update command to force the cluster engine to retry the allocation sequence on the unassigned shards: 'curl -X POST "localhost:9200/_cluster/reroute?retry_failed=true&pretty" -H "Content-Type: application/json"'
Step 5: Monitor the cluster stabilization state in real-time to watch the transition from RED to YELLOW as primary shards allocate: 'watch -n 1 "curl -s localhost:9200/_cluster/health | jq .status"'
Step 6: Once the primary shards are restored, force an indexing synchronization event to align any dropped data: 'curl -X POST "localhost:9200/hotel_rooms/_refresh?pretty"'
Step 7: Trigger a rolling restart of the dependent search microservice instances to clear any bad proxy connections or stale circuit breakers: 'systemctl restart search-api-service.service'

title: Missing Relational DB Index on High-Frequency Guest History Inquiries
triggers_and_scope:
1. Application Monitoring: 'Database Read Latency Spike > 3000ms'.
2. Database Engine Logs: 'pg_log: duration: 4210.512 ms statement: SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE customer_id = $1 AND status = 'ACTIVE' ORDER BY checkin_date DESC;'.
3. System Dashboard: Database engine host core processor utilization spikes to 100% capacity.
4. Scope: Widespread latency across customer mobile portals and the primary guest check-in portal.
issue_summary: As the historical 'bookings' database ledger table grows past millions of records, legacy application queries begin to slow down if they lack structural index support. During busy checking hours, thousands of active guest inquiries hit the service simultaneously. Without an explicit index on the target columns (`customer_id`, `status`), the relational database is forced to run sequential table scans (reading every row from disk), pinning host CPU resources and causing query queues to back up.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Connect to the primary transactional database node using the interactive administrative console interface: 'psql -h pg-master.internal.hotelapp.net -U db_admin -d hotel_reservations'
Step 2: Generate an explicit runtime execution plan for the slow query to confirm the presence of a sequential scan operation: 'EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM bookings WHERE customer_id = 998231 AND status = 'ACTIVE' ORDER BY checkin_date DESC;'
Step 3: Verify the output rows for structural flags containing 'Seq Scan on bookings'. This confirms the absence of functional database indexing structural configurations.
Step 4: Inject the missing database index layout onto the production system environment. Use the 'CONCURRENTLY' keyword to guarantee that the database engine builds the index structure without locking out incoming write operations: 'CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_bookings_customer_status_date ON bookings(customer_id, status, checkin_date DESC);'
Step 5: Track index creation completion by observing running system queries: 'SELECT query, state FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE query LIKE '%idx_bookings%';'
Step 6: Once built, execute an update on the table metrics to ensure the database query planner updates its route structures: 'VACUUM ANALYZE bookings;'
Step 7: Rerun the original execution query plan from Step 2 to verify the database engine is now performing an optimized 'Index Scan'.

title: Row-Level Deadlocks Resolution in High-Concurrency Inventory Mutations
triggers_and_scope:
1. Database Exception Logs: 'ERROR: deadlock detected' accompanied by 'DETAIL: Process 4102 waits for ShareLock on transaction 881203; blocked by process 4115'.
2. App Engine Logs: 'org.springframework.dao.DeadlockLoserDataAccessException: PreparedStatementCallback; SQL statement []; Deadlock detected; nested exception is org.postgresql.util.PSQLException'.
3. Scope: Critical impact on the 'inventory-manager' and 'booking-service' layer.
issue_summary: Deadlock conditions materialize when two concurrent reservation threads attempt to modify identical database records within the 'room_inventory' and 'customer_ledger' tables, but execute their update statements in reverse order. For example, Thread A locks Inventory Row X and requests Ledger Row Y, while Thread B locks Ledger Row Y and requests Inventory Row X. The relational database engine halts further progression to protect data integrity, picking one transaction to abort via a deadlock exception.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Extract the unique process identifiers (PIDs) involved in the deadlock loop by reviewing the detailed backend engine log files.
Step 2: Log into the target database node database instance console: 'psql -h pg-master.internal.hotelapp.net -U db_admin -d hotel_reservations'
Step 3: Query the current locking matrix to identify the blocking process that is causing the application layer to stall: 'SELECT blocked_locks.pid AS blocked_pid, blocking_locks.pid AS blocking_pid, blocked_activity.query AS blocked_statement, blocking_activity.query AS current_statement_causing_block FROM pg_catalog.pg_locks blocked_locks JOIN pg_catalog.pg_locks blocking_locks ON blocking_locks.locktype = blocked_locks.locktype AND blocking_locks.database IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blocked_locks.database AND blocking_locks.relation IS NOT DISTINCT FROM blocked_locks.relation JOIN pg_catalog.pg_stat_activity blocked_activity ON blocked_activity.pid = blocked_locks.pid JOIN pg_catalog.pg_stat_activity blocking_activity ON blocking_activity.pid = blocking_locks.pid WHERE NOT blocked_locks.granted;'
Step 4: Terminate the lower-priority transaction process block manually to release the held locks and allow the main application threads to complete: 'SELECT pg_terminate_backend(blocking_pid);'
Step 5: Execute an administrative tasks runner command to process the resulting failed application messages from the deadlocked queue: 'python manage.py process_failed_reservation_queue'
Step 6: Monitor the active locking states to confirm no further deadlock signatures persist: 'SELECT count(*) FROM pg_locks WHERE granted = false;'
Step 7: Check that the error rates on the booking system metrics dashboard have returned to zero.

title: Amazon S3 Rate Limiting Recovery via Path Partition Hash Hashing
triggers_and_scope:
1. Application File System Logs: 'com.amazonaws.services.s3.model.AmazonS3Exception: Status Code: 503, Error Code: SlowDown, Please reduce your request rate.'
2. Metric Tracking Dashboard: 'Invoice Storage Dispatch Drop Rate > 25%'.
3. Scope: Affects the invoice processing background queue, causing delays in emailing customer receipts upon checkout.
issue_summary: When thousands of guests check out simultaneously at 11:00 AM, the 'invoice-generator' service processes thousands of PDF documents per minute. If the application writes these objects to an Amazon S3 bucket using a simple sequential path layout (e.g., `/invoices/2026-06-16/invoice_10234.pdf`), it hits the same physical S3 storage partition. Amazon S3 enforces a limit of 3,500 PUT requests per second per prefix. Exceeding this threshold triggers 503 SlowDown rate-limiting errors, dropping file uploads.
step_by_step_instructions:
Step 1: Access the cloud deployment orchestrator parameter configuration panel or connect to the main application configuration maps.
Step 2: Modify the path construction environment parameters to introduce an MD5 or randomized hash prefix into the S3 key structure. This distributes the files across different physical S3 partitions.
Step 3: Update the configuration parameter values to turn on randomized pathing: 'export INVOICING_USE_RANDOMIZED_PREFIX=true' and 'export INVOICING_HASH_PREFIX_LENGTH=4'.
Step 4: Apply the updated parameters to the running cluster using the Kubernetes command-line interface tool: 'kubectl rollout restart deployment/invoice-generator-service -n production'
Step 5: Monitor the application container logs to verify the new naming convention is active: 'kubectl logs -f deployment/invoice-generator-service -n production | grep "Uploading to S3 bucket"'. The paths should now look like: `/invoices/a4f1-2026-06-16/invoice_10234.pdf`.
Step 6: Clear out the local disk queue where failed files were backed up during the throttling window: 'curl -X POST https://invoice-generator.internal.net/api/v1/queue/retry-failed-uploads'
Step 7: Check the AWS CloudWatch S3 metric panels to confirm that 503 error rates have dropped back down to 0%.
